questions.q: What is Rust's primary advantage over C/C++?
What does the 'ownership' system in Rust prevent?
What happens when a variable goes out of scope in Rust?
What is borrowing in Rust?
How many mutable references can exist to a value at the same time?
What is a 'lifetime' in Rust?
options: The scope for which a reference is valid

questions.q: Which keyword is used to define a function in Rust?
What is the Result type used for?
options: Error handling with Ok and Err variants

questions.q: What does the ? operator do in Rust?
options: Propagates errors by returning Err early

questions.q: What is a trait in Rust?
options: A shared behavior definition similar to interfaces

questions.q: What does 'match' do in Rust?
options: Pattern matching against values with exhaustive checking

questions.q: What is the difference between String and &str?
options: String is heap-allocated and owned; &str is a borrowed string slice

questions.q: What is an enum in Rust?
options: A type that can be one of several variants, optionally with data

questions.q: What is Option<T> used for?
options: Representing a value that may or may not exist (Some or None)

questions.q: What does 'cargo build' do?
What is a crate in Rust?
options: A compilation unit — either a binary or library

questions.q: What does the 'mut' keyword do?
Are variables mutable by default in Rust?
options: No, they are immutable by default

questions.q: What is a closure in Rust?
options: An anonymous function that can capture variables from its environment

questions.q: What does 'impl' do?
options: Defines method implementations for a struct or trait

questions.q: What is the Vec<T> type?
What does 'derive' do on a struct?
options: Automatically implements traits like Debug, Clone

questions.q: What is a macro in Rust?
options: Code that generates other code at compile time, invoked with !

questions.q: What does println! do?
options: Prints to stdout with a newline — it's a macro

questions.q: What is the 'move' keyword used for with closures?
options: Forces the closure to take ownership of captured variables

questions.q: What is unsafe Rust?
options: A block that allows operations the compiler can't verify as safe

questions.q: What is a smart pointer in Rust?
options: A data structure that acts like a pointer but has additional metadata and capabilities

questions.q: What does Box<T> do?
options: Allocates data on the heap and provides ownership

questions.q: What is Rc<T>?
options: A reference-counted smart pointer for shared ownership

questions.q: What is the Send trait?
options: Indicates a type can be safely transferred between threads

questions.q: What does 'cargo test' do?
options: Runs all test functions annotated with #[test]

questions.q: What is pattern matching used for?
options: Destructuring and branching based on the shape of data

questions.q: What is the difference between iter() and into_iter()?
options: iter() borrows elements; into_iter() takes ownership

questions.q: What is a module in Rust?
options: A way to organize code into namespaces using the mod keyword

questions.q: What does #[derive(Debug)] enable?
options: Printing the struct with {:?} format specifier

questions.q: What is a slice in Rust?
options: A reference to a contiguous sequence of elements in a collection

questions.q: What does 'pub' do?
options: Makes an item public and accessible from outside its module

questions.q: What is the HashMap type?
options: A collection that stores key-value pairs with O(1) lookup

questions.q: What does 'loop' do in Rust?
options: Creates an infinite loop that must be explicitly broken

questions.q: What is the 'if let' syntax used for?
options: Matching a single pattern concisely, ignoring others

questions.q: What is zero-cost abstraction?
options: Abstractions that compile to code as efficient as hand-written low-level code

questions.q: What is the 'use' keyword for?
options: Bringing items into scope to shorten paths

questions.q: What does 'struct' define?
options: A custom data type that groups related fields together

questions.q: What is the turbofish syntax ::<>?
options: Explicitly specifying generic type parameters on function calls

questions.q: What are generics in Rust?
options: Parameterized types that allow writing code for multiple types

questions.q: What is the 'where' clause used for?
options: Specifying trait bounds on generic types in a cleaner syntax

questions.q: What does 'dyn Trait' mean?
options: A trait object for dynamic dispatch at runtime

questions.q: What is the difference between Copy and Clone?
options: Copy is implicit bitwise copy for simple types; Clone is explicit and can be custom

questions.q: What does 'cargo fmt' do?
options: Formats Rust code according to style guidelines

questions.q: What is the async/await model in Rust?
options: Asynchronous programming using futures that are lazily evaluated
